Earning multiple Nobles on one turn.body:
According to the printed rules in my box, if I buy a gem bonus that makes me eligible for more than one noble to visit, I choose one and play continues. 

What isn't made clear though is what happens on my NEXT turn. Assuming no other player has earned a visit from the noble in question in the interim, do I automatically trigger a visit from that noble as I was (and remain) eligible from the previous turn? Or am I barred from accessing that noble for the rest of the game? 

Apologies if this has been answered elsewhere in the threads, but I couldn't find it!
In the scenario you describe you get the second noble on your next turn.

lots of bgg is not intuitive so it has to be discovered... here I used the search while on the rule forum and I searched for

noble -re:

the -re: usually strips out replies and looks at only the base post of a thread

Reserved cards - face down ?.body:
Hi, everyone.
Could not find it in the forums: is it allowed to keep your (up to three) reserved cards face-down ? Or are they supposed to be "public info" all the time ?
We have always played keeping reserved cards face down.  I will double check the rules in my copy.
I believe that they have to be face-down until they are purchased. This is to separate them from the player’s pool so they aren’t “accidentally” used to purchase cards during gameplay.
According to the rules, reserved cards "are kept in hand". We always interpreted this as being hidden from the other players.

So in my copy, nothing in the rules explicitly states reserved cards must be kept face down. However when it discusses how to reserve a card, it does mention that one method is to reserve from the top of a deck and do not show the other players the card.

(Just following up on what the rules show.)
We keep them face up since it was open information when they were purchased. But there is no justification for that in the rules.

The rules are ambiguous. My spouse and I find it more strategically satisfying to keep them face up. Face down would remove a lot of the most interesting player interaction and jockeying for position, in my view.
We've always interpreted that the reserve cards are face-down.

Oliver is right: the rules states that the card are kept in hand therefor are not public information.

(Side note: it is not fair play to take a visible card from the tableau before all players have seen it)

The rules on reserving a card:

The vast majority of games in which players have a "hand of cards" means that those cards are hidden from other players.  Otherwise they would be called something other than a "hand of cards."  If they were meant to be face-up on the table, they would be called something else, such as your "pile of reserved cards."  But the general consensus in the forums seems to be that reserved cards are face-down (in your hand), and your purchased cards are face-up (in your tableau).  If they were *all* kept face-up on the table, there is a much greater chance of mixing up the cards that you have already purchased with the cards that you have reserved but not yet purchased.

The Splendor rules make it clear that the cards you reserve from one of the face-down decks remain hidden.  There are other games where you are also able to draw from either a face-up pile, or a face-down pile, and the drawn cards go into your hidden "hand of cards" (Ticket to Ride springs immediately to mind).  In that game, as well as in Splendor, it would be too much trouble to separate out the Hidden but Trackable Information (HTI).  In Ticket to Ride, it makes little sense to keep a *hidden* hand of cards which you have drawn from the face-down deck, and a separate *visible* hand of cards which you have drawn from the face-up offering.  I feel the same way, to a lesser degree, about the reserve cards in Splendor.

In my games of Splendor, *all* reserved cards go face down.

Also, much more reading on the subject, here:

Also, from this six year old thread (), we have this:

Cheers!

Thank you, guys !
We will try face-down / hidden reserved cards, it makes sense for all the reasons you stressed. If my group disapproves it, we can always house-rule it otherwise.

Is gold tied to the card you reserved?.body:
Is gold tied to the card you reserved? 
I seem to recall that it wasn't and you can use the gold as a wild in any later turn to buy cards
But i played it today, and the owner was adamant it is tied to the card - and that you only use the gold as a wild if you managed to buy the reserved card another way; in which case the gold can be used.
I read the latest rules (although very briefly) from the publishers site but wasn't able to find it (probably from skim reading it)

Can someone confirm whether gold is tied to reserved cards or not, and if it is; how do you use gold as a wild for other cards you wish to purchase?

Thanks

Gold is indeed wild and can be used toward buying any card.  For example, if a card costs 1 green and 1 red, you can buy it with 1 green and 1 gold.  Or buy it with 2 gold.

Remember, too, that one cannot have more than 3 reserved cards.

no, you can use it on any purchase

and it does count against the 10 limit at end of your turn
Thats great,  i knew i had it right.  Thanks all

no, you can use it on any purchase

Correct. This is a common misunderstanding, and something I call out specifically when I teach the game.

Rules Complexity and Strategic Complexit.body:
Someone invented a "next-generation way of quantifying the difficulty of a game. A simple 1-5 rating in the same fashion as BoardGameGeek doesn’t tell the full measure of the difficulty of a game since it doesn’t identify if a game is difficult due to the rules or the strategy.

 is a two-dimensional rating scale where the rules difficulty and the strategic difficulty is measured individually.

The first number quantifies the rules complexity using a 1-5 scale. A 1-rating has very few rules and can be taught verbally in seconds (think: tic-tac-toe rules). A 5-rating has a very thick rules manual, many addendums, FAQs, rules charts, player aids, and a long rules explanation. (think: High Frontier)

The second letter quantifies the strategic complexity using an A through an E. An A-rating has very low strategy and may be either instant to grasp or be entirely luck-based. An E-rating has layers and layers of complexity and may take years to master. (think: Go or many 18xx titles)"

I've created a poll to see what other people think the rules complexity and strategic complexity would be for this game.

Geeklist with all Rules Complexity and Strategic Complexity Polls: https://boardgamegeek.com/geeklist/354554

End Game Scoring and Nobles.body:
I apologize if this is covered somewhere else, but there are too many threads and I could not locate anyone discussing the following question I have.

Simply put, do you keep the score you had at the end of your turn, or at the end of the game?

For Example:

3 player game.  Players A B C.  Player A had the first turn.
On Player A's turn, they manage to buy a card that also causes a noble to visit them on their turn, their total is 16 points (Horay!).  End of game triggered, everyone else goes since you play an equal number of turns.
Player B goes, they buy a card, their turn ends, they have 15 points (second place, dang).  
Player C goes, they also buy a card, and additionally, they have enough cards now that they can have the same noble visit them that player A took.  So they take that card for the additional 3 points, however they still only have a total of 14 points.  

Here's my question.  Since Player C took the noble that visited player A, is their score now 13 points instead of 16 points, meaning player B would actually win with 15 points?  Or does Player A win because on their turn they had 16 points.

Thank you to anyone who can explain the official ruling.  Originally I would play where the score would be reduced but we found that this made it more favorable to whoever went last in our games.
Nobles aren't taken away from players.  The first one to meet the required gems takes the noble at the end of their turn.
only 1st player to claim a noble scores points for it, they actually take the noble and put it in front of them
ninja'ed!  just wanted to add that even if you meet the requirements for more than one noble on your turn to claim, one can only claim one noble per turn
OK wow I can't believe we've been playing this wrong the whole time.... my family is going to be so upset with me

Cars required to gain more than one nobl.body:
My question is can the cards be used to obtain multiple Nobles 

In the picture attached the player has received one Noble that requires three green three blue and three red 

There is another Noble available that requires three Brown three red and three green 

Would the player be able to obtain this Noble as well if they add three Brown to their collection of cards, but using the exact same cards for the red and green they needed to obtain the first Noble?

You can obtain multiple nobles using the same cards. You're not spending resources. As long as you satisfied the requirements, you can get the nobles. The only limitation is that you can't get more than one noble per turn.
I'd say it's an important part of strategy to look for nobles with overlapping requirements and try to get multiples.

Can you take two gold if there are 4 or.body: Or can you only gain gold by reserving cards?

You can never take more than one gold on a turn.  The only way to acquire gold chips is use the option of reserving a card and taking a single gold chip.  A player can hold no more than 3 cards in reserve.  Gold is not acquired by taking chips from the bank the way other colors are obtained.
Man, if you could take two gold if there were four or more, the first player advantage would be insurmountable.
you also get no gold if you reserve a card and the gold supply is empty

You can take anything you want if your opponents aren't looking.

:b

And eat it. 
You could also be also victorious by making offers the other players can't refuse.

And eat it. 
You could also be also victorious by making offers the other players can't refuse.

This is what's missing in abstract games, the true cutthroat nature of busines!

Just like in 7 wonders when you "defeat" someone in battle, subsequently leaving their entire city and economy intact! ;)

Resolving a missed hand limit..body:
Suppose a player has more than ten tokens at the end of their turn, but nobody noticed. What rules do you guys use to resolve this when somebody does notice at a later point in time?

We are playing casually and don't want to punish the player too harshly if it was an honest mistake.
When playing casually: Ask them to return the extra tokens when you notice it.
If we notice at their next turn, then they have to get rid of the excess before playing.

It's tricky, depending on *when* you notice.

Suppose its during a 4-player game.  Player 1 takes too many tokens, and no one notices at the time.  Players 2 and 3 take their turns.  Then the error is discovered on Player 4's turn, and the excess tokens are returned.

But what if that would have altered Player 2's and Player 3's turns?  Perhaps Player 2 needed red, which wasn't available at the time, and Player 1 returns a red token when the mistake is discovered during Player 4's turn.

I guess you need to try to redo the last two turns as well.  Or at least allow Player 2, then Player 3, to exchange tokens (assuming they took tokens on their turns), based on the tokens that Player 1 just returned to fix their error.

Cheers!
Yes, when them having the extra tokens in principle limited others players, just ask if they'd have done something differently.
To prevent this, we're in the habit of announcing our chip count at the end of our turn when it gets above five. That prompts us to count every time.

Korean Pokémon Rules Translated to Engli.body: Took pictures of the rules through google translate and combined them in Photoshop. Not perfect and some of the translations are funky but you should be able to learn the game from it.

Differences from the original that I can tell:

18 points wins. If tied, the player with more trained cards (upgraded pokemon) wins.

Cards can be bought with balls or evolved (upgraded) if the player has the corresponding balls in tableau.

Legendary cards need at least one master ball (equivalent to gold in the original).

Yep, and the noble system is sort of replaced by evolving. The game also comes with Pokedex sheets so you can track what Pokemon you have caught over multiple games. I don't remember Splendor having any system for kidnapping merchants.

If I have an opportunity to claim a noble that would put me over 15 or not, that usually has no bearing on whether another player will score later on that turn and trigger endgame himself.   And if I'm the last player in a round and I can trigger an endgame, that means I win.

Looking at some other threads on the topic, notably this one: https://boardgamegeek.com/thread/1572740/splendor-nobles-what-downside/page/2

Gabor B. @uborka_original suggests the rule is there so that if you forget to take a noble you are entitled to, other players can't claim you chose not to take it.

Pete (thinks that makes sense)

I do not think that's a valid reason to pass on a noble even if you could per the rules. There are only two options how this round can play out:

a) Someone is in turn order before you. They either will already have 15+ points which means it is the last round. Or they have fewer than 15 which means you will win with the noble. 

b) Someone is in turn order after you which means they can either not overtake you in VP or gain 16 or more points which ends the game anyway without giving you another turn.  

Maybe you are forgetting that all players get an even number of turns, meaning the last player will be next to the starting player, not the one next to the player ending the game?

not sure how that would work in tournament play 🙂 playing "friendly" sure.

Are the other players here also required to point that out to the player?  

Seems like such a rule should only be in the "family friendly variant" section

b) Someone is in turn order after you which means they can either not overtake you in VP or gain 16 or more points which ends the game anyway without giving you another turn.

Hi!,

The rules in spanish say:
(and my confusion starts with the Game Start part, cuz I don't understant that like a "THE ROUND starts with..." 🥲)

Game Start part: "THE GAME starts with the youngest player and proceeds clockwise".
Game Over part: "When a player has 15 points (don't forget to count the nobles), THE ROUND in play continues, so that all players play the same number of turns."

Maybe I take (for my very notorious lack of reading comprehension) "THE ROUND in play" as from when a player gets the 15 points (or more) that trigger the end of the game and for me all the players take one last turn even if I'm the player at the right from the player who starts "the game" or... "the round..." x_x.

A note on terminology: In Splendor, a *Round* consists of all players taking a single *Turn*.  This is mostly consistent through most board games, but is not always the case.

Say you are playing a 3-player game, with players A, B, and C.  Player A goes first, followed by Player B, and Player C goes last.

Say, Player B triggers the end game by scoring 15 points on their 12th turn.  That means you will finish out the round and the game is over.  Player C will get one more turn (as it will be their 12th turn), and scores 16 points.

Player A doesn't get another turn, since they already took their 12th turn during the 12th and final round before Player B triggered end-game.

End result:  Player A finishes with fewer than 15 points on 12 turns.  Player B finishes with exactly 15 points on their 12th turn.  Player C wins the game by passing Player B in scoring on their final (12th) turn.

Games don't always last 12 rounds, but that seems to be somewhere around the average when I play the app.  Some games end in 10 rounds (or less).  Some end in 14 rounds (or more).  But the main takeaway is that you compare scores after all players have played the same number of rounds.

--As an aside, when playing the app and you reach the prerequisites to earn two Nobles on the same turn, it shows you which two Nobles you can claim, and you must choose one of them.  You will earn the other one automatically at the end of your next turn (provided someone else doesn't claim it before you).  So it is always good to look at other players' tableaus and first choose the Noble that other players are closer earning, so that the second Noble will most likely still be there next round for you to claim.  It does not let you *not* take a Noble that you have earned.

In brief: The player sitting to the right of the youngest player will always take the last turn of the game. (Assuming your started the game according to the rules.) 

For scoring, it does not matter where in turn order the player ending the game is as everyone gets the same number of turns (which will equal the number of rounds played).

Hi! Thanks! and yeah I understand that about the rounds, I have seen it in other games as you mention, but the wording of the rules in Spanish for Splendor confused me “a bit”haha.
So from now on I'll interpret it that way 😀
almost never... for gaves with a start player advantage or disadvantage it will always be the same start player each time for a given game within a small play group.

Reserved cards - face down ? [thread:353.body:
Hi, everyone.
Could not find it in the forums: is it allowed to keep your (up to three) reserved cards face-down ? Or are they supposed to be "public info" all the time ?
We have always played keeping reserved cards face down.  I will double check the rules in my copy.
I believe that they have to be face-down until they are purchased. This is to separate them from the player’s pool so they aren’t “accidentally” used to purchase cards during gameplay.
According to the rules, reserved cards "are kept in hand". We always interpreted this as being hidden from the other players.

So in my copy, nothing in the rules explicitly states reserved cards must be kept face down. However when it discusses how to reserve a card, it does mention that one method is to reserve from the top of a deck and do not show the other players the card.

(Just following up on what the rules show.)
We keep them face up since it was open information when they were purchased. But there is no justification for that in the rules.

The rules are ambiguous. My spouse and I find it more strategically satisfying to keep them face up. Face down would remove a lot of the most interesting player interaction and jockeying for position, in my view.
We've always interpreted that the reserve cards are face-down.

Oliver is right: the rules states that the card are kept in hand therefor are not public information.

(Side note: it is not fair play to take a visible card from the tableau before all players have seen it)

The rules on reserving a card:

The vast majority of games in which players have a "hand of cards" means that those cards are hidden from other players.  Otherwise they would be called something other than a "hand of cards."  If they were meant to be face-up on the table, they would be called something else, such as your "pile of reserved cards."  But the general consensus in the forums seems to be that reserved cards are face-down (in your hand), and your purchased cards are face-up (in your tableau).  If they were *all* kept face-up on the table, there is a much greater chance of mixing up the cards that you have already purchased with the cards that you have reserved but not yet purchased.

The Splendor rules make it clear that the cards you reserve from one of the face-down decks remain hidden.  There are other games where you are also able to draw from either a face-up pile, or a face-down pile, and the drawn cards go into your hidden "hand of cards" (Ticket to Ride springs immediately to mind).  In that game, as well as in Splendor, it would be too much trouble to separate out the Hidden but Trackable Information (HTI).  In Ticket to Ride, it makes little sense to keep a *hidden* hand of cards which you have drawn from the face-down deck, and a separate *visible* hand of cards which you have drawn from the face-up offering.  I feel the same way, to a lesser degree, about the reserve cards in Splendor.

In my games of Splendor, *all* reserved cards go face down.

Resolving a missed hand limit. [thread:3.body:
Suppose a player has more than ten tokens at the end of their turn, but nobody noticed. What rules do you guys use to resolve this when somebody does notice at a later point in time?

We are playing casually and don't want to punish the player too harshly if it was an honest mistake.

If we notice at their next turn, then they have to get rid of the excess before playing.

It's tricky, depending on *when* you notice.

Suppose its during a 4-player game.  Player 1 takes too many tokens, and no one notices at the time.  Players 2 and 3 take their turns.  Then the error is discovered on Player 4's turn, and the excess tokens are returned.

But what if that would have altered Player 2's and Player 3's turns?  Perhaps Player 2 needed red, which wasn't available at the time, and Player 1 returns a red token when the mistake is discovered during Player 4's turn.

I guess you need to try to redo the last two turns as well.  Or at least allow Player 2, then Player 3, to exchange tokens (assuming they took tokens on their turns), based on the tokens that Player 1 just returned to fix their error.

Cheers!
Yes, when them having the extra tokens in principle limited others players, just ask if they'd have done something differently.
To prevent this, we're in the habit of announcing our chip count at the end of our turn when it gets above five. That prompts us to count every time.

Korean Pokémon Rules Translated to Engli.body: Took pictures of the rules through google translate and combined them in Photoshop. Not perfect and some of the translations are funky but you should be able to learn the game from it.

Differences from the original that I can tell:

18 points wins. If tied, the player with more trained cards (upgraded pokemon) wins.

Cards can be bought with balls or evolved (upgraded) if the player has the corresponding balls in tableau.

Legendary cards need at least one master ball (equivalent to gold in the original).

Yep, and the noble system is sort of replaced by evolving. The game also comes with Pokedex sheets so you can track what Pokemon you have caught over multiple games. I don't remember Splendor having any system for kidnapping merchants.

Gabor B. @uborka_original suggests the rule is there so that if you forget to take a noble you are entitled to, other players can't claim you chose not to take it.

Pete (thinks that makes sense)

I do not think that's a valid reason to pass on a noble even if you could per the rules. There are only two options how this round can play out:

a) Someone is in turn order before you. They either will already have 15+ points which means it is the last round. Or they have fewer than 15 which means you will win with the noble. 

b) Someone is in turn order after you which means they can either not overtake you in VP or gain 16 or more points which ends the game anyway without giving you another turn.  

Maybe you are forgetting that all players get an even number of turns, meaning the last player will be next to the starting player, not the one next to the player ending the game?

so other players when it gets to their turn and could claim the nobel has to point out and give it to the player who should have claimed it?

Are the other players here also required to point that out to the player?

Hi!,

The rules in spanish say:
(and my confusion starts with the Game Start part, cuz I don't understant that like a "THE ROUND starts with..." 🥲)

Game Start part: "THE GAME starts with the youngest player and proceeds clockwise".
Game Over part: "When a player has 15 points (don't forget to count the nobles), THE ROUND in play continues, so that all players play the same number of turns."

Maybe I take (for my very notorious lack of reading comprehension) "THE ROUND in play" as from when a player gets the 15 points (or more) that trigger the end of the game and for me all the players take one last turn even if I'm the player at the right from the player who starts "the game" or... "the round..." x_x.

A note on terminology: In Splendor, a *Round* consists of all players taking a single *Turn*.  This is mostly consistent through most board games, but is not always the case.

Say you are playing a 3-player game, with players A, B, and C.  Player A goes first, followed by Player B, and Player C goes last.

Say, Player B triggers the end game by scoring 15 points on their 12th turn.  That means you will finish out the round and the game is over.  Player C will get one more turn (as it will be their 12th turn), and scores 16 points.

Player A doesn't get another turn, since they already took their 12th turn during the 12th and final round before Player B triggered end-game.

End result:  Player A finishes with fewer than 15 points on 12 turns.  Player B finishes with exactly 15 points on their 12th turn.  Player C wins the game by passing Player B in scoring on their final (12th) turn.

Games don't always last 12 rounds, but that seems to be somewhere around the average when I play the app.  Some games end in 10 rounds (or less).  Some end in 14 rounds (or more).  But the main takeaway is that you compare scores after all players have played the same number of rounds.

--As an aside, when playing the app and you reach the prerequisites to earn two Nobles on the same turn, it shows you which two Nobles you can claim, and you must choose one of them.  You will earn the other one automatically at the end of your next turn (provided someone else doesn't claim it before you).  So it is always good to look at other players' tableaus and first choose the Noble that other players are closer earning, so that the second Noble will most likely still be there next round for you to claim.  It does not let you *not* take a Noble that you have earned.

In brief: The player sitting to the right of the youngest player will always take the last turn of the game. (Assuming your started the game according to the rules.) 

For scoring, it does not matter where in turn order the player ending the game is as everyone gets the same number of turns (which will equal the number of rounds played).

Hi! Thanks! and yeah I understand that about the rounds, I have seen it in other games as you mention, but the wording of the rules in Spanish for Splendor confused me “a bit”haha.
So from now on I'll interpret it that way 😀
